emergent_themes.description: Students living with parents who handle groceries/cooking feel free to spend discretionary income on delivery as experimentation rather than necessity
Cashback mechanics create stronger loyalty than price sensitivity, driving repeat orders to 'use credits' and justify subscription cost
Participants may understate home cooking frequency in interviews to appear more 'modern' or delivery-oriented than actual behavior shows

coding_summary: P009 presents a significant say-do gap between interview claims and diary reality. While claiming to cook 'once a week' in interview, diary shows daily home cooking as 'usual routine.' His delivery behavior is more strategic than frequent - enabled by family food security, driven by subscription loyalty mechanics (5% cashback), and triggered by social occasions or convenience needs. Strong price comparison behavior across platforms, with £3-4 threshold needed to overcome Uber One loyalty. Campus activations and student-specific deals highly valued.
